content.text: Every vector database ships a nearest-neighbour search over dense embeddings. That works well when the user paraphrases a concept differently from how it was written — but it struggles with exact-match requirements: a specific product SKU, a function name, a proper noun spelled in an unusual way. BM25 (the statistical sparse-retrieval algorithm underlying most search engines) fills that gap exactly. Hybrid search runs both paths in parallel and merges their ranked lists into a single result set that is stronger than either alone.

attrs.alt: Architecture diagram: User Query splits into BM25 sparse path (tokenize → inverted index → BM25 ranked results) and Dense path (embed → ANN HNSW → dense ranked results), both merged through RRF into Final Hybrid Results.

content.text: BM25 (Best Match 25) is a bag-of-words ranking function that scores a document by how often the query terms appear in it, discounted by document length and saturated so a term appearing 100 times does not outweigh one appearing 10 times by 10×.

attrs.latex: \text{score}(D, Q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{IDF}(q_i) \cdot \frac{f(q_i, D) \cdot (k_1 + 1)}{f(q_i, D) + k_1 \cdot \left(1 - b + b \cdot \frac{|D|}{\text{avgdl}}\right)}

content.text: — average document length in the corpus

BM25 works on an inverted index: at index time, every token is mapped to the list of documents containing it along with the term frequency. At query time, the matching document lists are intersected or union-ed and scored in O(matched docs) time — typically microseconds even for millions of documents.
BM25 is exact for tokens. If the query contains
, BM25 will score it high regardless of embedding space position. Dense retrieval may not, especially for rare or synthetic tokens outside the model's training distribution.

Dense retrieval encodes both the query and every document into a high-dimensional float vector using a bi-encoder (e.g. text-embedding-3-large, all-MiniLM-L6-v2, or a domain-fine-tuned model). At query time, an approximate nearest-neighbour (ANN) index (HNSW, IVF, DiskANN) finds the vectors closest by cosine or dot-product distance — typically top-100 candidates in a few milliseconds even across millions of vectors.
The distance function for cosine similarity:
Dense retrieval captures semantic similarity: a query about
even without shared tokens, because a good embedding model maps them to nearby regions of the vector space.
Why Combine Them — the Complementarity Argument
No if not in training data

Neither signal dominates universally. The practical win of hybrid search over each individual method typically ranges from 3–10 nDCG points on standard RAG benchmarks (BEIR, MS-MARCO), and is most pronounced on corpora that mix structured identifiers with free-form prose — exactly the shape of most enterprise knowledge bases and code repositories.
Merging Ranked Lists: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF)

The two retrievers return independent ranked lists. To merge them without needing to normalise scores across incompatible scales (BM25 scores are corpus-dependent; cosine distances are bounded but not comparable to BM25), most systems use Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF):
is the set of ranked lists (one per retriever),
is a smoothing constant (commonly 60). A document that ranks 1st in both lists scores
. A document that ranks 1st in one and 10th in another scores
. Documents absent from a list contribute 0 for that list.
No score normalisation required — only ranks matter.
Outliers in one list (e.g. BM25 score of 500 vs typical 10) do not dominate.
Documents appearing in both lists receive a bonus — the
Documents unique to one list still appear if they rank highly enough in that list.
When scores from both retrievers can be normalised to [0, 1], a simpler weighted blend is practical:
divide each score by the max score in that retriever's result list. Fast but sensitive to outliers.
pass BM25 scores through a sigmoid to squash them to (0, 1).
standardise each list to zero mean, unit variance. More robust but requires more computation.
Most production systems and framework APIs default to RRF because it avoids the normalisation problem entirely.
Weaviate has first-class hybrid search support. It runs BM25 over a built-in Okapi BM25 inverted index and dense ANN over its HNSW vector index, then merges with RRF (default) or weighted fusion.

import weaviate
from weaviate.classes.query import HybridFusion

client = weaviate.connect_to_local()
collection = client.collections.get("ResearchPaper")

# alpha=0.5  →  equal weight; fusion=RRF (default)
result = collection.query.hybrid(
    query="transformer self-attention mechanism 2024",
    alpha=0.5,
    fusion_type=HybridFusion.RANKED,   # or HybridFusion.RELATIVE_SCORE
    limit=10,
    return_metadata=weaviate.classes.query.MetadataQuery(score=True),
)
for obj in result.objects:
    print(obj.metadata.score, obj.properties["title"])

client.close()
for score normalisation + weighted blend instead of RRF.
objects — one for each vector field — combined via a

from pymilvus import MilvusClient, AnnSearchRequest, WeightedRanker, RRFRanker

client = MilvusClient(uri="http://localhost:19530")

# Sparse BM25 request (requires a BM25EmbeddingFunction on a SPARSE_FLOAT_VECTOR field)
bm25_request = AnnSearchRequest(
    data=[sparse_query_vector],     # output of BM25EmbeddingFunction
    anns_field="sparse_vector",
    param={"metric_type": "IP"},
    limit=50,
)

# Dense request
dense_request = AnnSearchRequest(
    data=[dense_query_vector],      # output of your embedding model
    anns_field="dense_vector",
    param={"metric_type": "COSINE", "params": {"ef": 64}},
    limit=50,
)

# RRF merge (k=60 default)
results = client.hybrid_search(
    collection_name="papers",
    reqs=[bm25_request, dense_request],
    ranker=RRFRanker(k=60),        # or WeightedRanker(0.3, 0.7)
    limit=10,
    output_fields=["title", "abstract"],
)
for hit in results[0]:
    print(hit.score, hit.entity.get("title"))
The sparse field stores a scipy sparse vector produced by Milvus's built-in
. The function indexes the corpus and computes query vectors at search time. For
, Milvus normalises each retriever's scores internally before applying the weights.
Implementing Hybrid Search with LangChain EnsembleRetriever
applies RRF across any list of retrievers — mixing a BM25Retriever with a FAISS or Chroma vector store retriever in a few lines.

from langchain_community.retrievers import BM25Retriever
from langchain_community.vectorstores import FAISS
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain.retrievers import EnsembleRetriever

# 1. Build a BM25 retriever from raw docs
bm25_retriever = BM25Retriever.from_documents(docs, k=10)

# 2. Build a dense retriever
embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")
faiss_store = FAISS.from_documents(docs, embeddings)
dense_retriever = faiss_store.as_retriever(search_kwargs={"k": 10})

# 3. Combine with RRF — weights control contribution
#    (LangChain EnsembleRetriever uses weighted RRF internally)
ensemble = EnsembleRetriever(
    retrievers=[bm25_retriever, dense_retriever],
    weights=[0.4, 0.6],   # sparse weight, dense weight
)

docs_result = ensemble.invoke("transformer self-attention 2024")
for doc in docs_result[:5]:
    print(doc.metadata.get("title"), doc.page_content[:120])
Under the hood, LangChain's EnsembleRetriever calls each retriever in parallel, collects the ranked lists, and runs RRF with the provided weights as rank-frequency multipliers. The
parameter of each retriever controls how many candidates enter the fusion step; setting
too low (< 20) can starve the merge and hurt recall.

from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex, StorageContext
from llama_index.core.retrievers import VectorIndexRetriever, BM25Retriever
from llama_index.core.query_engine import RetrieverQueryEngine
from llama_index.retrievers.bm25 import BM25Retriever as LI_BM25Retriever
import Stemmer

# Build vector index
index = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(documents)

# Dense retriever
vector_retriever = VectorIndexRetriever(index=index, similarity_top_k=10)

# BM25 retriever (requires llama-index-retrievers-bm25)
bm25_retriever = LI_BM25Retriever.from_defaults(
    docstore=index.docstore,
    similarity_top_k=10,
    stemmer=Stemmer.Stemmer("english"),
    language="english",
)

# Hybrid: fuse with RRF
from llama_index.core.retrievers import QueryFusionRetriever

hybrid_retriever = QueryFusionRetriever(
    retrievers=[vector_retriever, bm25_retriever],
    similarity_top_k=10,
    num_queries=1,               # 1 = no query expansion
    mode="reciprocal_rerank",    # RRF
    use_async=True,
)

query_engine = RetrieverQueryEngine.from_args(retriever=hybrid_retriever)
response = query_engine.query("scaled dot-product attention mechanism")
print(response)
) requires domain knowledge and ideally offline evaluation against a labelled set of queries.
BM25 is robust with few tokens

import numpy as np
from sklearn.metrics import ndcg_score

# For each (query, relevant_doc_ids) test case:
def evaluate_alpha(retriever_fn, test_cases, alphas):
    results = {}
    for alpha in alphas:
        ndcgs = []
        for query, relevant_ids in test_cases:
            hits = retriever_fn(query, alpha=alpha, k=10)
            hit_ids = [h["id"] for h in hits]
            # binary relevance vector
            y_true = [1 if hid in relevant_ids else 0 for hid in hit_ids]
            y_score = list(range(len(hit_ids), 0, -1))
            if sum(y_true) > 0:
                ndcgs.append(ndcg_score([y_true], [y_score]))
        results[alpha] = np.mean(ndcgs)
    best = max(results, key=results.get)
    print(f"Best alpha={best:.2f}  nDCG@10={results[best]:.4f}")
    return results
BM25 Wins: When to Lean Sparse
There are query classes where BM25 reliably outperforms dense retrieval, sometimes by a large margin. Recognising them allows setting
), people names with unusual spelling, scientific compound names (
). Embeddings often conflate syntactically similar but semantically different identifiers.
— dense models often ignore negation and return docs about FAISS.
BM25 is language-agnostic at the token level; multilingual embeddings add noise for monolingual corpora.
Dense Wins: When to Lean Semantic
Dense retrieval outperforms BM25 in scenarios where the surface form of the query diverges from the surface form of the answer:
— BM25 needs all three tokens to appear; dense embeds the concept.
multilingual models (mE5, LaBSE) retrieve across language barriers; BM25 stops at the token boundary.
when documents use varied vocabulary (journalism, books), dense recall is substantially higher than BM25.
Pattern 1 — Pre-compute sparse vectors at ingest time (recommended for large corpora):

# At index time: store BM25 corpus stats alongside dense embeddings
from rank_bm25 import BM25Okapi
import pickle, numpy as np

def build_hybrid_index(docs):
    # Dense
    embeddings = embed_model.encode([d["text"] for d in docs])

    # Sparse: fit BM25 on corpus
    tokenised = [d["text"].lower().split() for d in docs]
    bm25 = BM25Okapi(tokenised)

    # Persist both
    np.save("dense_vectors.npy", embeddings)
    with open("bm25.pkl", "wb") as f:
        pickle.dump(bm25, f)
    return bm25, embeddings

# At query time
def hybrid_search(query, bm25, faiss_index, all_ids, alpha=0.5, k=10):
    tokens = query.lower().split()

    # BM25 scores for all docs
    bm25_scores = bm25.get_scores(tokens)

    # Dense top-100
    qvec = embed_model.encode([query])[0]
    D, I = faiss_index.search(qvec[np.newaxis], 100)
    # Map to full-corpus scores (unseen docs get 0)
    dense_scores = np.zeros(len(all_ids))
    for i, idx in enumerate(I[0]):
        dense_scores[idx] = 1 - D[0][i]    # cosine → similarity

    # Normalise both to [0, 1]
    bm25_norm = bm25_scores / (bm25_scores.max() + 1e-9)
    dense_norm = dense_scores / (dense_scores.max() + 1e-9)

    combined = alpha * dense_norm + (1 - alpha) * bm25_norm
    top_k = np.argsort(combined)[::-1][:k]
    return [(all_ids[i], combined[i]) for i in top_k]
Pattern 2 — Query routing (alpha adapts per query):
Hybrid Search in a RAG Pipeline

from langchain_community.retrievers import BM25Retriever
from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings, ChatOpenAI
from langchain.retrievers import EnsembleRetriever
from langchain.chains import RetrievalQA
from langchain_core.documents import Document

# Assume `docs` is a list of LangChain Document objects

# 1. BM25 sparse retriever
bm25 = BM25Retriever.from_documents(docs, k=15)

# 2. Dense vector retriever
store = Chroma.from_documents(
    docs,
    OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small"),
    collection_name="papers",
)
dense = store.as_retriever(search_kwargs={"k": 15})

# 3. Hybrid retriever (weights: 35% sparse, 65% dense)
hybrid = EnsembleRetriever(
    retrievers=[bm25, dense],
    weights=[0.35, 0.65],
)

# 4. RAG chain
llm = ChatOpenAI(model="claude-sonnet-4-6", temperature=0)
qa_chain = RetrievalQA.from_chain_type(
    llm=llm,
    retriever=hybrid,
    return_source_documents=True,
)

response = qa_chain.invoke({"query": "What is scaled dot-product attention?"})
print(response["result"])
for doc in response["source_documents"]:
    print("  ↑", doc.metadata.get("title"))
Comparison: BM25 vs Dense vs Hybrid
BM25 is case-sensitive by default. If you index lowercased text but query with mixed case, recall drops. Apply the same tokenisation pipeline at index time and query time.
Setting k too low for the retrievers feeding hybrid merge.
, RRF has very few candidates to merge — you may get 5 results at most. Set
Using different embedding models at index and query time.
The dense retriever requires the same model (or at least the same embedding space) for index and query. Swapping models requires re-indexing all documents.
The default is reasonable but leaves significant recall on the table for domain-specific corpora. Run an offline evaluation on a labelled set.
Not parallelising the two retrieval paths.
BM25 and dense retrieval are independent — run them concurrently. In Python, use
or separate threads to halve the latency of the merge step.
Mixing weighted blend with unnormalised BM25 scores.
BM25 scores are corpus-dependent and unbounded. Using
without normalisation lets BM25 dominate regardless of alpha. Use RRF or normalise first.
It is robust, requires no normalisation, and is the default in Weaviate and Milvus. Switch to weighted blend only if you have labelled data showing a specific alpha outperforms it.
Benchmark alpha on your actual query distribution.
Use nDCG@10 against a small held-out set of 50–200 labelled queries. Even a rough grid search over alpha ∈ {0.2, 0.4, 0.5, 0.6, 0.8} usually identifies the right regime.
Run BM25 and dense retrieval in parallel.
They are completely independent — parallelising them keeps hybrid latency close to
Apply metadata filters before or during hybrid search.
Pass the same filter predicate to both the BM25 retriever and the dense ANN search; do not post-filter the merged list, as that breaks RRF rank assumptions.
If the average document length or vocabulary distribution shifts (e.g. after ingesting a new domain), re-fit the BM25 statistics and consider re-benchmarking alpha.
Use a fine-tuned domain embedding model for the dense path.
The gains from domain adaptation often exceed the gains from tuning alpha. A general model plus well-tuned alpha is a good baseline; a fine-tuned model with the same alpha often outperforms it.
Hybrid search combines BM25 sparse retrieval with dense ANN retrieval
to cover both exact-match and semantic-match query types.
— it requires no score normalisation and is robust to outliers.
tune it on a labelled query set rather than guessing. Most RAG workloads benefit from dense-biased alpha (0.6–0.75).
BM25 excels at rare tokens, exact entities, and code identifiers
; dense excels at paraphrase, semantic similarity, and cross-lingual queries.
Weaviate, Milvus, LangChain EnsembleRetriever, and LlamaIndex QueryFusionRetriever
all provide first-class hybrid search APIs that handle the merge internally.
